speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date-- oh, sorry. Today's date, November 27, 1995. Survivor Manya Feldman, nee Barman, M-A-N-Y-A F-E-L-D-M-A-N, B-A-R-M-A-N. Interviewer, Marilyn Feingold--

speaker: interviewer
text: They can read. Just say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Oak Park, Michigan, country, United States, language, English. My name is Marilyn Feingold, M-A-R-I-L-Y-N F-E-I-N-G-O-L-D. Today's date is November 27, 1995. I have the privilege, today, of interviewing Manya Feldman, in Oak Park, Michigan, United States of America. Can you please tell me your name and then spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Manya Feldman, M-A-N-Y-A F-E-L-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? And can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Mania Barman, M-A-N-I-A B-A-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. OK. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on February the 5th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would make you how old now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Dabrowica, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Dabrowica for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-O-M-B-R-O-W-I-T-Z-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Manya, we're going to begin by talking about your life before the war started. And I'm wondering, could you-- could you describe the-- the community that you lived in? You know, how big was it? What it looked like, what the people were like, a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK. It was a shtetl, a very small town. And the Jewish population was about-- the general population was 5,000. The Jewish population was 3,000. The Jewish population lived in one area, specifically. And we didn't mingle too much with the gentile population. We were all like segregated. We had really not much in common. The-- we had 5 synagogues in a little shtetl of 3,000 Jews. And everything was geared to-- towards religion. The majority, I would say 95%, were Orthodox. This is how I was raised. I was-- my education started in a Hebrew day school, called Tarbut. And I-- so did-- my siblings attended the same school. And everything was in an Orthodox way. We celebrated the holidays. We went to-- the schul was our center, the synagogue. The rabbi was our psychologist, psychiatrist. We went to him for advice. And I was born, simultaneously, and I spoke four languages, my mother tongue, which was Yiddish, Hebrew. Well, then, when I started school, also Polish, which-- which was the language of the land, and Russian, sort of like a mixture of Russian and Ukrainian. That was the general language of the population, because the population was mostly Ukrainian. We were five children in our family. I had a sister, an older sister, her name was Leah. Then came-- then I came. My-- my brother was after me. His name was Yidel. I had a sister, Freidel, and a sister, Esther. We were four girls and one boy and my parents, naturally. My father was a small merchant. We had a grocery store. And everybody pitched in to help out in the business. We had a very, very happy life, a happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a happy childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very happy childhood. We were-- we were an huge family, probably close to 200 people. My father comes from a family of five brothers. And each one had about five children, four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Berl Barman. And my mother's name was Sonia Barman. And we kept together. Friday night, everything stopped. My father went to schul. We observed the Shabbat. The Shabbat meal was a very happy occasion. We sang the zemirot, the Shabbos songs. And everything was circled around religion. And also, we were also tuned into Zionism. Our dream was, one day, to immigrate to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were you involved in any Zionist groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was-- I was, ever since I can remember. I must've been about maybe seven, eight years old. I was in-- I belonged to a-- a Zionist organization called the Halutz, which is 'pioneers.' And we used to get together and sing songs and celebrate the fact that someday we'll all be united in our own homeland. Because Poland was a bad place to live in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you have experiences of antisemitism, directly, before the war started, that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had-- we had neighbors. We actually lived on the outskirts of town. We didn't get involved, except in business. They used to come and buy merchandise from us. And the outbursts was always remarks about the Jews are cheating them, and the Jews are no good. The Jews are a threat to their Polish society. And in school, actually, up until I was 14 years old, I was in my Hebrew school. So everything was in Hebrew. As a matter of fact, there were no gentiles. But after that-- I graduated in 1937. So-- and I was born in '23, so it made me 14 years old. Since higher education was very expensive, and my father couldn't afford. And in the city, we didn't have any higher education. We had to go out of town to another city. That was extremely costly. So there was not much, really, to do for youth, except learning a trade. And the trades were very, very limited. There was seamstresses. And for boys, they were carpenters and tailors. And this was about it. There was not much work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, in order not to sit idle at home and not do nothing, I repeated another year in the Polish school. And this is where I came across antisemitism, even on the part of the teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to call the Jews bad names. They used to say that the Jews, in general, are a menace to society. They are no good. They are a bad influence.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naturally, it had an influence on the children. Also, they had prayer in school. So the sin-- the soon-- as soon as they came in, the first thing we did is they had to pray. And naturally, when they prayed, they-- they had to kneel. So the Jewish children did not kneel. And as-- as a matter of fact, that's how we were, right away, outstanding. Right away, they knew, who is Jewish and who is not Jewish. I see. Do you remember like when the rise of Nazism-- hearing about the Nazis coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that happened yet much before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Much?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the last prime minister was Jozef Pilsudski. He was a very good man. Under his regime, the Jews had a very good life. There was antisemitism, but it was sort of suppressed. It wasn't that bad. But as soon as he died, they started getting closer in German-- to Germany. Don't forget, it was-- he died in 1935. Hitler was on the rise. And he started to infiltrate the Polish ranks by sending spies and also infiltrating their own people into the Sejm. And the Sejm was like the House of Representatives, here, or the Senate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had people that served in that Sejm. And the first thing we knew, they forbade our kosher slaughtering of the animals. They said that it's inhumane. And that we could not do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible, because we then-- we do not-- we-- we didn't eat non-kosher foods. So this was the first sign, generally, that we observed about antisemitism on the part of the government. And also, they had-- they used to put out sign, in Polish, meaning, svoy do svegah, go to your own. In front of the stores, they had signs saying, don't buy from Jews. You-- you have to buy from Poles. Very openly, very openly, the-- the influence of Nazism protruded into Poland very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing the expression, 'Polska dla Zydow,' and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polska dla Zydow is not-- it wasn't. Polska dla Zydow means, 'Poland for the Jews.' Polska was not dla Zydow. The Zydzi--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Zydzi out of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hear Zydzi dla Palestynie?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Palestynie, Zydzi-- that's right. They wanted to chase us out. Except, we didn't have our own homeland. A lot of people, at that time, immigrated, even it were the-- in an illegal-- illegal way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family make any attempts to leave the country at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's dream was, when the children will grow up, just to send them away from Poland. He, himself, could not go, because my father was an invalid from the First World War. He had all his four fingers were shot off. So he had only one thumb on his right hand. And as an invalid, somebody that is disabled, they wouldn't let him in into no country. But his dream was, when his children will grow up, they will send them away, most likely to Israel or to America. We had an uncle here. My father's brother imitated-- immigrated here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, all right. At-- at this point, we were talking, you-- you-- they-- they wouldn't let you do the kosher slaughtering of animals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: What other--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the next thing that they did that things started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in general, to tell you the truth, the-- the-- the Jews were not allowed into higher education places. You had to be real privileged, which Jews were not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had our own higher schools of learning and that were private. They were very expensive. So Jews did not, as a rule, got to gymnasium. Forget about college. So we had our own. And we paid our own way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you saw a lot of it, more, much more in the streets. It was very open. There was an organization called the [NON-ENGLISH]. They were the youth, who marched in the street and openly called Jewish names and says, go to-- go to Palestine. This is not your place. We don't want you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do you remember when Poland was invaded by the-- the Germans? Do you remember where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. To begin with, let's back up a little bit in history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland was divided into two in 1939, when Stalin and Hitler made a pact to divide Poland. So the eastern part-- we lived on the eastern part-- so we lived under the Russian regime, under communism. And that was a tremendous transition, because they also-- they were nice. There was no antisemitism. There was no open antisemitism. They were saying things in-- in the background. But if somebody called a Jew a derogatory name, he was put in jail for that. So this thing was not in the open. The-- The school system changed. They-- they-- right away, they disposed of the Hebrew school, because that was like connected with Zionism. So we couldn't-- there were no Hebrew school anymore. But what they did, instead, they had a Yiddish school. But it was funny. The Yiddish school wasn't according to the Yiddish that we knew. They-- they did it different. And it lasted for a very short time. We started continuing at our education, because their-- the Russian education was on a high level. And at that time, they had already gymnasium, which means post-elementary. In other words, I could-- I started going, already, to the eighth grade. And if the war wouldn't have interrupted, I would have graduated with a high school diploma. Life changed, naturally. We didn't have any comers, like the stores were confiscated. My father couldn't do any business. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your family do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was barred from even getting a job, because he was classified as a small merchant. And this means that-- it's called, 'a speculator.' In other words, he was cheating people, because he was in business. You know, and in Russian, the-- under the Russian regime, everything belonged to the government. The way we survived is because we had an awful lot of merchandise saved up, spared from their-- from our store. We had grain. And it was-- it was meager. We had to stay in line to get bread, milk. Meat was practically non-available. And we wouldn't buy the non-kosher meat either. We were also barred, under communism, to slaughter in a kosher way. So we did it in a clandestine way. And we always had meat. Dairy products we had, because we had-- we always had our own cows, sometimes had two cows. You know, the-- the-- the way the Jews were separate-- situated, they had their own stable. And they had a cow. And this is how we had our own milk, butter, cheeses. And vegetables, we had a big-- in our case, we had a huge garden. So we planted our own vegetables. The-- the-- the girls, which was myself, my-- my sister, and I, and my brother, we were going to school. And we were also working a little bit in-- in offices. There was an awful laugh-- a lot of time spent standing in line for food. But life was-- naturally, it wasn't a democracy. It was communism. It was entirely different. But it was bearable. It was OK. It wasn't that bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, what-- then, in 1941, when the Second World War started, we-- we had a very-- a-- an outstanding, tremendous assistant by the Russian government. They prepared railway cars, trains. And they said, to the Jewish population and, for that matter, for to everybody, whoever wants to evacuate and avoid remaining with a German government, you can do so. As a matter of fact, a lot of people did, in our town, those who were connected with the communist regime in a private way. And I really begged my father. Somehow, I had a premonition that it will not be right. Because the news trickled down not too much. We didn't have any radios-- television, for sure not. And it trickled down that the situation for the Jews wasn't so good. When I approached my father, and I said, look, there's a train here. And you can take whatever stuff-- naturally, you have to leave everything, your house, and-- and-- and whatever possessions you have. He said, no. It's not a life under communism. In retrospect, if we would have known what was awaiting for him, he would have ran, very fast. My mother was in the-- in their city, in World War I, when the Germans occupied, and the war was between Russia and Germany. And she couldn't have enough praise of the German people, how polite and how nice they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was under the impression that this is the way it was going to continue. And she says, nothing wrong. We can-- we can-- we can survive here. Let's see. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had a-- an entire week. And I'll never forget it. And I'll forever be grateful to the Russian government for assisting those Jews, who wanted to-- to be evacuated. And after the week, it was like there was no government at all. The Russians left. And the Germans didn't occupy yet. So finally, the Germans came in. We did not have any experience of war going on. It was a little town, with no significant spots, like industry or anything like this. We heard airplanes flying. And you know, the-- the victory over Poland was very swift. Within one day, they were all over. And one shiny day, it was in July. I think it was on a Sunday, if I'm not mistaken. We were sitting on the porch, and the German drove in-- the Germans drove into town on tanks and in-- on trucks. And the-- the Ukrainian population was elated. They were so happy. They met them with flowers. We Jews sat quietly, on the sidelines, trying to see what's going to happen. Because we didn't know how to act. We lived on the main street. And we were sitting on our porch, watching. And as they drove in, the-- the-- the-- Ukrainians said, oh God, finally, we got rid of these Russians. And finally, we will have back our democracy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were sitting on the porch, in-- in one split second, my father was grabbed. And he was grabbed by two people that he was doing business. And he was dumbfounded. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They started grabbing Jewish males. My-- my brother was about-- at that time, he was-- he was about 14 or 15 years old. He ran into the house. So right away, we asked him to go on the attic and hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we ran into the house, and we locked our doors. And we put the shutters down. And we were just sitting and shivering. What's going on? He asked the guys that grabbed him, what-- what's happening? What? I mean, I know you. He called them by their names. Why are you grabbing me like this, with such anger? And he said, wait, you dirty Jew. You'll find out in a minute. Your time is up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we stayed in the house. And we didn't know what to do. So we decided, whatever will happen, we're going maybe to endanger ourselves, but we have to go and see what happened to our father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother and I ventured out to the center of the city. And when we came there, the sight was unbelievable. There were about a couple hundred of Jewish males, just males, sitting on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the center of the city had a square. This was the style of the cities. The square was the marketplace. They were sitting. And in front of them, had all the-- all their weapons, with a-- whatever you call them. They had the bullets already ready, just ready to push the button, you know, to kill them. And we started-- my mother started running, with other women, and trying to see what's going on. So they ran to the priests-- there was a Ukrainian priest and a Polish priest-- and asked them, maybe they could intervene.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's happening? They went to the mayor of the city, who was Polish. And they told them that they grabbed these males, to put them up as hostages, as a preventive in case something happens to the parts of the German army. So this is when they'll kill them out. But since they intervened, so the priest and the mayor went to the German authorities. And they said, listen, these are not the communists. The communists all escaped. They-- they went back to Russia. And these are the good-- the good Jews. Why don't you let them go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed there till about-- it started getting dusk outside. And they let them go, except they held 22 hostages. And the way they picked them is-- was at random. They were looking at more sophisticated, intelligent faces, people with glasses. Not too many wore glasses. And they took the 22. And the rest of them, they let go. At the same time, they gave the gentile population, in an open manner, the freedom to go and loot and-- and grab and-- and steal and take whatever their heart desires from the Jewish homes. And they did. They were looting. They were taking. They were grabbing, whatever came to their heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they go into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- we were, as I said, we were on the outskirts. They were doing it in the center. But by the same token, the bureaucrats, the bureaucrats, as the Germans are, they had a befehl, which means the command, that-- that you can only loot till midnight. And then it has to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were looting. And a-- it-- a-- a very awkward thing happened. They caught a gentile fellow. He was looting, and it was close to midnight or a little bit past midnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: They arrested him And they put him up together with the hostages. We didn't know what-- my father was not among the hostages, by the way. We didn't know what's going to happen to the hostages. And they stayed three days, somewhere, in a-- in a separate house. And they put this gentile fellow, and they put him up together. After-- on the third day, they said something is-- something is happening. They took out the hostages, and they told them to dig a-- a hole, to dig like a grave, in the center of the city. And everybody was sure that this is-- they're going to shoot the-- the 22 hostages. And that's where they're going to bury them. But that-- but that wasn't the case. They shot, in the open, in front of the entire city, they shot this gentile fellow for disobeying orders of the Germans. And they buried him right there. And they let the 22 hostages go. But that wasn't the beginning of the picnic that we had with the Germans. That was the beginning of the real, horrible, atrocious, terrible life that we started experiencing. Every single day, they had all kind of announcements, put out on walls, put out on a-- on a electric poles, that the Jews had to dispose of, giveaway their silver, any gold, any jewelry. They had to give them away. They had to bring them to the center and give them to the Germans. And by-- at the same time, they organized a Judenrat. A Judenrat is-- is the head of the Jewish population. And they were like the-- the liaisons between the Jewish population and the German. They were the ones to take orders and-- and-- and pass it on to the Jewish people. The Judenrat had to organize a labor force. And how-- that's how the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people who were in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them first? Because it's a small town?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a small town. We knew the people. They were nice people. But they had to obey the orders of the Germans. And they organized a-- a labor force. And the labor force was composed of anybody, girls or boys or men or old persons, from the age of 16 and up, up to the age of whatever. The-- those-- the people didn't live that long. They were in their 50s and their 60s. And every day, we had to report to work. So go-- coming back to the-- to the commands that they gave for the Jewish population--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, at this point, we have to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I can scratch my nose.
